content:
as of https://roc.zulipchat.com/#narrow/stream/316715-contributing/topic/Pull.20Request.20for.20Review/near/528497566 we no longer have the wildcard * type variable name. Instead, we generate type variable names like so:

empty_list : List(elem)
empty_list = []

reverse : List(elem) -> List(elem)

Previously, the empty list would have been typed List(*) instead.

One thing I liked about List(*) that the current design doesn't have is that you could tell at a glance (and also when talking out loud) whether a variable was bound (like in List(elem) -> List(elem) or unbound (like in List(elem) by itself where that's the whole type annotation. Notice how I felt the need to clarify just now?)

I had an idea: what if we used the underscore prefix for unbound type variables? e g.

empty_list : List(_elem)
empty_list = []

reverse : List(elem) -> List(elem)
the idea is that it's "unused" (which it is!) so we use that naming convention, whereas in reverse the two elem variables actually are used (by each other) so they don't get the underscore
I think this might be a way to get the benefits of * with the benefits of named vars at the same time
what to others think?
And it would be a warning if it was used.
yeah, and that could go both ways - could also give a warning if it wasn't used and yet didn't have an underscore!
Ok
Seems reasonable...but no major preference either way
Interesting. Subtle but helpful. I like that it is consistent with other usages of _ident
Could have the formatter automatically fix that up, right?
I don't think so, that would require type information which I didn't think the formatter used?
Or are you thinking of a heuristic or similar keeping track of type vars in type annotations?
For annotations the formatter could manually deal with it
Just has to scan the whole type annotation for repeated names
I think a parsed annotation would have enough info
Oh ok. I'm not sure if we should or shouldn't.
Yeah, in this case at least world be super local cause you're emotions are short
Am I wrong if I read _ in types as "inferred type"? By analogy _name for me is "inferred type with a name". In the same way how _ is unused value and _name is its named version.
_elem looks good, but I would wait to see if people really need it.
